evidence: Strictly layered backend: Domain/ is a pure leaf (avg_fan_out 0, avg_fan_in 21); every directory edge in the coupling_matrix flows downward (Features/Messages, Auth, Data, Rooms, Moderation -> Domain) with no reciprocal or bidirectional edges.

Vertical-slice feature folders (Messages, Rooms, Moderation) with clean intra-module decomposition: MessageReader (read side), MessageService (write side), MessageEnricher (projection), HighlightsService (ranking) each own a single responsibility and depend only downward on Data/Domain/Realtime/Storage.

Shared concerns are centralized in the right owner: HighlightsCache.Key constant, a single HighlightsService.Score() formula reused by both the public highlights feed and ModerationController analytics, and RealtimeNotifier/MessageChangeDto owned by Realtime and consumed downward by Features (Features -> Realtime, correct direction).

Features/Messages -> Auth (16 edges) is limited to the cross-cutting User.GetUserId() extension and Roles constants (MessagesController.cs:25,76,122; HighlightsController.cs:41) — a legitimate downward dependency on a shared auth utility, not a boundary inversion.

Features/Moderation -> Features/Messages (7 edges) reuses HighlightsService.GetSnapshotAsync/Score for the admin top-posts panel (ModerationController.cs:142-145) — intentional reuse of the ranking snapshot, downward and acyclic, avoiding a re-walk of every thread.

Frontend is strictly acyclic and layered: types.ts (leaf) <- api.ts <- appSupport.ts (shared base) <- appHooks.ts/realtime.ts/components/views <- App.tsx (composition root). No view imports another view, no component imports a view, and base modules (appSupport, api, types, roles, realtime) have no back-edges to higher layers (verified by grep).

No import-time side effects or shared mutable global state coordinating modules: the only statics are a single-flight SemaphoreSlim and the DI MemoryCache key in HighlightsService, both intentional. No compatibility shims or cross-package duplication.

Minor: Domain/ conflates pure entities (Message, Room, Vote, etc.) with static text utilities (Slug, Text) under one hub folder counted as 21 importers each; this slightly blurs the leaf's role but has negligible blast radius at 2,611 LOC.
cross_module_architecture.issues_preventing_higher_score: Domain/ mixes pure persistence entities with static string utilities (Slug.cs, Text.cs) in the same top-level hub folder that every module imports; the conflation is benign here but means the most-imported boundary owns two distinct roles, so the dependency hub is not perfectly single-purpose.

evidence: Backend uses a clean, domain-aligned decomposition: Domain/ (entities + small value helpers Slug/Text), Data/ (AppDbContext, DbSeeder, design-time factory), Auth/, Email/, Realtime/, Storage/, and Features/{Messages,Rooms,Moderation}. A new engineer can explain why each top-level folder exists without reading much code.

A small, consistently applied pattern set: thin controllers delegate to feature services; domain errors use result-object records (MessageCreateResult/DeleteMessageResult/ReportMessageResult/RoomCreateResult) instead of exceptions; controllers translate result codes to HTTP. Pattern repeats across Messages and Rooms.

The Messages feature shows a deliberate CQRS-style split with coherent ownership: MessageService.cs (commands/writes), MessageReader.cs (read queries + pagination), MessageEnricher.cs (DTO projection / N+1 avoidance), HighlightsService.cs (cached ranking). XML doc comments on MessageReader/MessageEnricher/HighlightsService explicitly state each file's role, e.g. 'Read side ... Commands live in MessageService'.

Public surface per feature is small: each Features/* folder exposes a controller + service(s) + a DTOs file; Realtime owns its wire DTO (MessageChangeDto in RealtimeNotifier.cs) which is consumed by MessageService — a sensible ownership choice.

Program.cs is a clean composition root: DI registration, auth/OAuth wiring, rate-limit policies, security headers, and startup migration/seed all in one entry point; static local helpers (GetAllowedOrigins, ValidateJwtOptions, GetRateLimitKey) keep it readable.

Frontend mirrors the same discipline: views/ (page components), components/ (reusable chrome/messages/overlays), and flat support modules with clear single responsibilities — api.ts (HTTP), realtime.ts (SignalR), appHooks.ts (custom hooks), appSupport.ts (routing + pure helpers + types), roles.ts (permission predicates).

ModerationController.cs (379 LOC) performs all analytics aggregation, report triage, ban/role mutations, and user listing directly in the controller with private query helpers (LoadTrendAsync/LoadTopRoomsAsync/LoadTopPostsAsync) — it has no ModerationService, unlike its Messages/Rooms siblings which split controller from service. A mild layering inconsistency, though Moderation is read/admin-mostly and the controller is the only consumer.

An empty Controllers/ directory persists at server/Sheshi.Api/Controllers (a scaffolding leftover) alongside the feature-foldered controllers — a small straggler that momentarily implies a flat-controllers organization that the codebase abandoned.

codebase_analysis.md is a high-quality reference doc but its Directory Structure section has drifted from runtime: it documents the frontend as monolithic views.tsx (861 lines) and ui.tsx (532 lines) single files, but the code is actually split into views/ and components/ directories; it lists App.tsx as 536 lines (actual 589); its Gaps section still claims 'Not a git repository', 'No Dockerfiles for the API', and a README referencing a stale alb_sheshi/ path — all no longer true.
high_level_elegance.issues_preventing_higher_score: ModerationController carries feature-service responsibilities inline while sibling features (Messages/Rooms) split controller from service, so the layering pattern is not uniform across all features; an empty leftover Controllers/ directory and a stale Directory-Structure section in codebase_analysis.md (frontend described as monolithic views.tsx/ui.tsx files that no longer exist) slightly erode the trustworthiness of the structure/reference docs.

evidence: Backend C# is uniformly conventional: every controller (AuthController, MessagesController, RoomsController, ModerationController, PresenceController) uses primary constructors, [ApiController]/[Route]/[EnableRateLimiting] attributes, PascalCase types/methods with Async suffix, camelCase params, and namespaces that mirror folder paths.

DTOs follow one pattern everywhere: positional `record` types in per-feature `*Dtos.cs` files with `*Dto` suffix for responses and `*Request` for inputs (RoomDtos.cs, AuthDtos.cs, ModerationDtos.cs, MessageDtos.cs).
Service results follow a uniform sealed-record-with-static-factory protocol (MessageCreateResult/DeleteMessageResult/ReportMessageResult in MessageService.cs, CreateRoomResult in RoomService.cs).

Frontend non-component modules are consistently camelCase filenames with named exports and zero default exports (api.ts, appHooks.ts, appSupport.ts, realtime.ts, roles.ts, types.ts); components grouped thematically with named exports (chrome.tsx, messages.tsx, overlays.tsx).

Convention outlier: in frontend/src/views/, Auth.tsx, Moderation.tsx, and Profile.tsx are PascalCase but feeds.tsx is lowercase, despite all four holding top-level routed view components (App.tsx:25-28 imports them side-by-side, App.tsx:466-556 routes Home/RoomView/ThreadView identically to Profile/ModerationView/AuthPage).

Structural variation (not a defect): Moderation has no service layer (analytics logic lives in ModerationController) while Messages/Rooms split controller+service; this is reasonable read-heavy variation and the rubric's skip list discourages forcing unification.
convention_outlier.issues_preventing_higher_score: The frontend views/ directory mixes PascalCase (Auth.tsx, Moderation.tsx, Profile.tsx) and lowercase (feeds.tsx) file names for files of identical role, creating a small style island a developer scanning the import block at App.tsx:25-28 would notice.

evidence: Service layer uses a uniform Result-record idiom for expected domain failures: MessageCreateResult, DeleteMessageResult, ReportMessageResult (MessageService.cs:187-205) and CreateRoomResult (RoomService.cs:92-96) all expose static factory methods (Created/Failed/NotFound/Forbidden) carrying a string error code.

Read paths consistently return null for not-found (MessageReader.GetMessageAsync/GetThreadAsync, RoomService.GetBySlugAsync, TokenService.RotateRefreshTokenAsync) and controllers translate null->NotFound()/Unauthorized() uniformly (MessagesController.cs:32-33,49-52; RoomsController.cs:23-24).

Controllers translate service Result/null into HTTP responses with a single error envelope shape `{ error = "CODE" }` using SCREAMING_SNAKE codes (ROOM_NOT_FOUND, PARENT_ROOM_MISMATCH, INVALID_MODE, RATE_LIMITED); MessagesController.cs:80-83, RoomsController.cs:33-38, HighlightsController.cs:18.

Storage layer throws a typed domain exception (ImageStorageException with a Code property, ImageStorageException.cs) carrying machine codes; MessageService catches it specifically and converts ex.Code into a Result.Failed(ex.Code) (MessageService.cs:45-48) — a clean throw->Result boundary between layers.
LocalFileImageStorage.SaveAsync wraps file I/O in try/catch that cleans up the temp file and `throw;` re-raises the original exception with no context loss (LocalFileImageStorage.cs:64-68).

Background and startup boundaries are deliberate top-level handlers: RefreshTokenCleanupService separates OperationCanceledException (shutdown) from generic Exception (log warning + retry next interval) (RefreshTokenCleanupService.cs:44-51); Program.cs startup catch logs then rethrows (fail-fast); ValidateJwtOptions and DbSeeder throw InvalidOperationException with descriptive messages for misconfiguration.

AuthController.SendConfirmationEmailAsync swallow is documented best-effort ('a broken mail server must not block registration') and logs a warning with the exception (AuthController.cs:64-78) — intentional, not silent.

Top-level pipeline gives predictable failure surface: app.UseExceptionHandler returns `{ error = "INTERNAL_ERROR" }` and the rate limiter returns `{ error = "RATE_LIMITED" }` (Program.cs:66-70,281-286), AddProblemDetails registered.

Frontend mirrors the backend: a single ApiError class carries status + code, one send() boundary parses the server `{error|errors}` envelope into ApiError, and call sites distinguish 404 from generic failure (api.ts:31-58; App.tsx:253-261). localStorage/clipboard catches in appSupport.ts are intentional fallback boundaries.

The strategy_by_directory scan signal (result_type in Auth/Messages/Moderation/Rooms, throws in Storage/Data, returns_null in Domain/readers) reflects intentional per-layer strategy, exactly the variation the rubric instructs to skip — not accidental inconsistency.
error_consistency.issues_preventing_higher_score: Two unsuppressed I/O boundaries write to the database without a feature-local failure strategy, leaving them to bubble to the generic 500 handler: TokenService.CreateAuthResponseAsync/RotateRefreshTokenAsync and the report/vote SaveChanges paths have no transient-failure handling, and the only documented public error-contract surface is the in-code SCREAMING_SNAKE codes (no OpenAPI/ProducesResponseType annotations enumerating them). These are minor and consistent with the codebase's deliberate fail-to-500 posture, but they keep the dimension just short of exemplary.

evidence: Backend service/reader split is named by intent: MessageService holds commands (CreateMessageAsync, UpvoteAsync, SoftDeleteAsync, ReportAsync), MessageReader holds reads (ListRoomMessagesAsync, ListRepliesAsync, GetThreadAsync); a developer can predict responsibility from the class name.

Domain helpers read as verbs that match behavior: TokenService.RotateRefreshTokenAsync, RevokeAllForUserAsync, HashRefreshToken; HighlightsService.GetSnapshotAsync/Score/FreshTieBreakScore; MessageEnricher.EnrichAsync; ResolveRootAsync.

Boolean/predicate names are honest: IsBanned, IsActive, HasValidSignature, HasClientSecret, canModerate/canAdmin/hasRole — every Is*/Has*/Can* returns a bool and no Get* method mutates state (verified by grep across server/).

Frontend names communicate intent without ceremony: subscribeToRealtime, useRealtimeRefresh, buildShareTarget, copyShareTarget, requireAuth, patchThread, findReplyNode, hotScore, authorAccent, timeAgo.
Consistent C# casing (PascalCase public members/types, camelCase locals/params) and consistent camelCase in TS; no convention drift between layers.
Minor: api.ts private helper dispatch() is a slightly generic verb for 'fetch with auth headers' (the public wrapper is the clearer send()) — isolated, 2-use, low impact.
Intentional Albanian route literals (dhoma/tema/profili/moderim) in parseRoute/roomPath are a deliberate localized URL vocabulary, not naming drift.
naming_quality.issues_preventing_higher_score: A private fetch helper named dispatch() in api.ts uses a slightly generic verb where the codebase otherwise favors precise names, and a few realtime params use messageId where the value is conceptually a thread/root id; both are trivial and isolated but keep naming just short of fully exemplary.

evidence: Only two I* interfaces exist (IImageStorage, IEmailSender) and both are genuine external-boundary adapters: IEmailSender is replaced with CapturingEmailSender in AuthFlowTests.cs (real test seam), and LocalFileImageStorage wraps the filesystem with magic-byte validation and atomic temp-file writes (swappable for S3/blob). Both fall under the rubric's explicit skip list.

Service classes carry real policy, not delegation: MessageService.CreateMessageAsync (Features/Messages/MessageService.cs:15) does validation, depth-capping/reparenting, image storage, and realtime/cache side effects; TokenService.RotateRefreshTokenAsync (Auth/TokenService.cs:38) implements atomic single-winner revocation and replay-detection. No method merely forwards to a repository.

Controllers are thin but earn their existence by translating domain Result records into HTTP status codes and applying auth/role/ban checks (MessagesController.cs:69-86, RoomsController.cs:33-38, ModerationController.Ban revokes refresh tokens) — not pass-through delegation.

Read/write concerns are split into focused collaborators (MessageReader, MessageEnricher, HighlightsService) each with a distinct responsibility documented in the class summary; MessageEnricher batch-loads in 4 queries to avoid N+1, HighlightsService centralizes the single scoring formula + single-flight cache.
Options objects (StorageOptions, JwtOptions) are small and fully consumed by their single owner; no wide context/option bag is plumbed broadly while methods use few members.
The 19 mechanical indirection_hotspots are EF Core/LINQ fluent chains (.Where().OrderBy().Take().ToListAsync()), not abstraction-layer indirection — a false positive for this dimension.
Minor: RoomService.NormalizeSlug (Features/Rooms/RoomService.cs:89) is a one-line private wrapper that only forwards to Slug.Normalize, adding no policy — a trivial, isolated thin-delegate helper.
abstraction_fitness.issues_preventing_higher_score: One trivial pass-through helper (RoomService.NormalizeSlug forwards to Slug.Normalize with no added behavior); abstraction count is so minimal that there is essentially no test-seam diversity beyond the two boundary interfaces, so the layer design is good but lightly exercised as an abstraction strategy.

evidence: Frontend (frontend/package.json) has 5 production deps and every one is used: chart.js (views/Moderation.tsx), lucide-react (7 files), @microsoft/signalr (src/realtime.ts), react and react-dom (src/main.tsx).
Single HTTP layer: src/api.ts uses native fetch; no axios or competing client. Single charting lib (chart.js, no recharts/d3/victory). No date libraries at all, so no moment+dayjs-style overlap.

Backend Sheshi.Api.csproj has 8 PackageReferences, all exercised in code: AddGoogle/AddMicrosoftAccount/AddApple/AddJwtBearer/AddOpenApi/UseNpgsql all present in Program.cs; Identity.EntityFrameworkCore via AppDbContext.
Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Design is correctly scoped with PrivateAssets=all and build-only IncludeAssets, so it is a design-time tool dependency rather than a runtime drag.
package-lock.json resolves react and react-dom to a single version (19.2.7); the multiple 'react' version strings seen are transitive peer-dependency ranges, not an actual conflict.

Minor: backend ASP.NET Core line packages mostly pin to 10.0.8 but a few drift on patch (AspNet.Security.OAuth.Apple 10.0.0, Microsoft.AspNetCore.OpenApi 10.0.7, Npgsql.EntityFrameworkCore.PostgreSQL 10.0.2). These are separately-versioned vendor packages on the same major line, so this is cosmetic patch spread rather than a real version conflict.
dependency_health.issues_preventing_higher_score: Minor patch-version drift across the backend ASP.NET Core 10.0.x package set (10.0.0 / 10.0.2 / 10.0.7 / 10.0.8) means a developer auditing versions sees inconsistent pins, though none represents an actual incompatibility.

evidence: MessageService.CreateMessageAsync (MessageService.cs:15-97) reads as a clean linear pipeline: guard clauses for empty/too-long body, early-return validation for room/parent, scoped try/catch only around the image I/O boundary, then a small distinct branch tree (parent null / over-depth reparent / normal) that computes parentId+depth. Branches are necessary and distinct; the reparent branch carries a WHY comment.

MessageReader.cs uses bounded helpers (NormalizeLimit, EncodeCursor/DecodeCursor, ResolveRootAsync with a HashSet cycle guard, BuildReplyNodes recursion) — extraction level is balanced, no micro-fragmentation, recursion is inherent to the tree.

TokenService.RotateRefreshTokenAsync (TokenService.cs:38-64) handles the stolen-token replay and atomic-revoke race precisely with early returns and a row-count check; side effects are explicit and local.

LocalFileImageStorage.SaveAsync (LocalFileImageStorage.cs:16-71) is precise low-level craft: temp-file + File.Move atomicity, streaming with a running byte cap, magic-byte signature validation on the first chunk, and a single catch that cleans up the temp file and rethrows.

HighlightsService.GetSnapshotAsync uses a textbook double-checked single-flight pattern; LoadStatsAsync walks ancestors with a per-message seen-set guard. Dense but the complexity is inherent to the ranking domain and well-commented.
PresenceTracker mutations are all bounded inside a single lock with consistent dictionary-cleanup-on-empty handling across JoinRoom/LeaveRoom/Disconnect.

ModerationController.TryParseStatus (ModerationController.cs:290-300) is the one local rough edge: a switch computes the `parsed` out value, then the return statement re-evaluates `status.ToLowerInvariant()` against the same three string literals (a third time), duplicating the branch logic and ignoring the computed result.

Frontend internals are equally tidy: realtime.ts subscribeToRealtime uses a disposed flag to guard async start/stop teardown; appHooks.ts effects all return correct cleanup. messages.tsx ReplyTree repeats `props.savedIds.has(node.message.id)` inline four times (lines 138-144) — minor noise, standard JSX.
low_level_elegance.issues_preventing_higher_score: ModerationController.TryParseStatus triple-evaluates status.ToLowerInvariant() and duplicates its branch set in the return rather than deriving success from the switch; minor inline-expression repetition in messages.tsx ReplyTree. These are isolated rough edges, not systemic, but they keep this below the 95+ band reserved for uniformly proportionate internals.

evidence: Service layer returns explicit result records (MessageCreateResult, DeleteMessageResult, ReportMessageResult, CreateRoomResult) with static factory methods; controllers translate the single Error code field to an HTTP status in one obvious place (MessagesController.CreateMessageAsync lines 80-85, RoomsController.Create lines 33-38) — handoffs are minimal and unsurprising.

Error propagation across the storage->service->controller chain is predictable: LocalFileImageStorage throws ImageStorageException(code), MessageService catches it and folds the code into MessageCreateResult.Failed (MessageService.cs lines 45-48), the controller maps it to a response. One direction, one obvious seam.

JSON boundary translation is centralized once in Program.cs (SnakeCaseLower naming policy + enum converter, lines 30-35), so the snake_case wire contract the frontend depends on (room_id, parent_id, display_name) has a single source of truth rather than per-DTO mapping.

Read/write are split into MessageReader and MessageService that both compose the shared MessageEnricher projection seam and the shared static MessageReader.ResolveRootAsync; orchestration reads as composition of collaborators (CreateMessageAsync: persist -> enrich -> evict cache -> notify realtime, MessageService.cs lines 88-96).

Frontend api.ts funnels every call through one send() seam with transparent 401 refresh-and-retry and a single ApiError carrying the backend error code (lines 42-58); the cross-runtime error contract mirrors the backend's { error } / { errors } shape.

Friction: error codes cross the service->controller seam as bare strings matched by literal (result.Error == "ROOM_NOT_FOUND", "PARENT_ROOM_MISMATCH"); the discriminator is an implicit string contract rather than an enum, so a typo silently falls through to the generic branch.
Vestigial seam: RealtimeNotifier.MessageChangedAsync(Guid roomId, Guid? threadId, ...) overload has zero callers (all four call sites use the MessageChangeDto overload) — a dead boundary surface.

Cross-language rule duplication: appSupport.ts hotScore reimplements HighlightsService.Score's recency constants (42 / pow(age+1,0.95) + 14 / pow(age+1,0.45)) with no link between the two; deliberate (client re-sort cannot share C# code) but the divergence is undocumented.

Presence seam is shape-asymmetric: the REST endpoint returns a snake-cased Dictionary<string,int> while SignalR pushes a PresenceDto, so the frontend RealtimePresenceUpdate/PresenceUpdate type hedges with both roomId and room_id.
mid_level_elegance.issues_preventing_higher_score: Service->controller error contract is stringly-typed (literal-matched codes), one dead notifier overload, and a recency-scoring rule duplicated across the C#/TS boundary without a documented link. These are isolated frictions on an otherwise clean, consistent set of seams.

evidence: Integration suite (CoreApiTests, AuthFlowTests, RealtimeStorageModerationTests, SeedAdminTests, CorsTests, SmokeTests) runs against a real Postgres via Testcontainers + WebApplicationFactory<Program>, exercising the full startup pipeline (migrations + role/room seeding) — no DB mocking, so tests validate real behavior rather than stubs.

Critical server paths are covered behaviorally: auth (register/login/refresh rotation + token-theft family revocation + logout + lockout-after-5-failures + confirm-email + forgot/reset revoking prior sessions), RBAC for room creation, message post/reply/nesting, depth-cap reparenting, vote idempotency, report idempotency, cursor keyset pagination, thread tree assembly, highlights ranking, moderation roles/ban/role-grant, SignalR 'changed' events + presence counts, and image upload with magic-byte sniffing/size-cap/type rejection.

UnitTests.cs gives a fast, dependency-free layer for pure logic the HTTP suite only hits indirectly: Text.Clip (Theory), Slug.Normalize (Theory incl. unicode/edge inputs), HighlightsService.Score/FreshTieBreakScore ranking invariants, PresenceTracker join/leave/disconnect semantics, ClaimsPrincipalExtensions.GetUserId parsing, LocalFileImageStorage save/reject/size paths.

Tests assert HTTP status + JSON DTO shape (locally-redeclared records with [JsonPropertyName]) and observable invariants, not implementation internals — low test-production coupling; refactors that preserve the API contract won't break them. Zero snapshot/Verify-style tests (0% snapshot, not >50%).

ModelParityTests asserts EF FK delete-behaviors and the keyset pagination index/filter directly against the model — a deliberate guard for the Supabase->EF port. EnvTemplateTests guards .env.example contract.

Frontend React/TS SPA (frontend/src: api.ts, realtime.ts, appHooks.ts, components/, views/) has ZERO automated tests and no test runner configured in package.json — one entire client-side layer is unvalidated.

CoreApiTests.Threads_store_root_depth_and_feeds_page_by_cursor relies on `await Task.Delay(5)` (line 215) to separate CreatedAt of two posts so cursor ordering is deterministic — a small timing reliance that could theoretically flake under coarse clock resolution.

CoreApiTests shares one fixture DB across its class (IClassFixture); the author handles isolation explicitly via GUID-unique emails/labels and documents the shared-room caveat — a managed tradeoff, not uncontrolled cross-test state.
test_strategy.issues_preventing_higher_score: The frontend SPA has no automated tests at all, leaving a whole client-side layer (api client, realtime, hooks, views) unvalidated; and a 5ms Task.Delay introduces a minor timing reliance in the pagination ordering test.

evidence: Controllers are highly uniform: every controller uses primary-constructor DI, [ApiController]+[Route("api/...")], ActionResult<T> return types, a trailing CancellationToken ct parameter, and [EnableRateLimiting] partitioned read/write/auth/moderation. A developer can predict the next endpoint's shape from any example.

Service-layer error contracts are consistent: command methods return small sealed result records (MessageCreateResult, DeleteMessageResult, ReportMessageResult, CreateRoomResult) with static factory methods (Created/Failed/NotFound/Forbidden), and controllers translate them to HTTP codes the same way; read methods return nullable DTOs mapped to NotFound().
Pagination is uniform: CursorPageDto<T> plus (limit, cursor, ct) ordering in both MessageReader.ListRoomMessagesAsync and ListRepliesAsync.

RealtimeNotifier exposes two public overloads of MessageChangedAsync; the (Guid roomId, Guid? threadId = null, ct) overload has zero callers (all four call sites in MessageService.cs use the MessageChangeDto overload) -- a dead, inconsistent-shape overload on the notifier surface.

HighlightsService.Score has an 8-parameter public static overload (upvotes, branchVotes, directReplies, descendants, isReply, createdAt, activityAt, now) alongside a Score(HighlightStats, now) convenience overload; the wide primitive list exceeds the >5-param guideline though it is a pure single-purpose scoring function.

The two [HttpPost("messages")] actions distinguished by [Consumes] JSON vs multipart are an intentional, framework-supported content-negotiation overload that normalizes into one CreateMessageAsync -- not a defect.

The sync/async-mix signal reflects sync helpers (Score, EncodeCursor/DecodeCursor, TryParseStatus, Snapshot) coexisting with async public methods, which is normal; no genuinely blocking sync call sits on an otherwise-async public API.
api_surface_coherence.issues_preventing_higher_score: One dead public overload (RealtimeNotifier.MessageChangedAsync(Guid roomId,...)) leaves an unused, inconsistent shape on the notifier surface; HighlightsService.Score's 8-parameter primitive overload is a mild width rough edge. Both are isolated and do not undermine the otherwise strongly predictable controller/service surface.

evidence: Permission strings are fully centralized in the Roles static class (Domain/Enums.cs) and used everywhere: [Authorize(Roles = Roles.Admin)] (RoomsController.cs:27, ModerationController.cs:233), [Authorize(Roles = Roles.ModeratorOrAdmin)] (ModerationController.cs:13), and IsInRoleAsync(user, Roles.Admin/Moderator) (MessagesController.cs:76,122-123). No magic role literals exist in any auth check; the only raw 'user'/'admin' strings are username-fallback defaults, not authorization.

Consistent [Authorize] placement: every mutating endpoint is protected (MessagesController write actions lines 55,62,88,102,114,131; AuthController logout line 114; MeController class-level line 10; RoomsController.Create line 27). Read endpoints (room messages, threads, highlights, room list, presence) are intentionally public for a forum and pass User.GetUserId() through as a nullable caller id.

Ban enforcement is layered and consistent across the trust boundary: at token mint (AuthController.Login:99 returns 403 ACCOUNT_BANNED; ExternalCallback:227), at refresh rotation (TokenService.RotateRefreshTokenAsync:53 rejects banned users), and at write controllers (MessagesController:73,95,138). Banning also revokes all active refresh tokens (ModerationController.Ban:216-218) so sessions die immediately.

Authorization is cleanly delegated into the service layer: MessagesController.SoftDelete computes canModerate from roles and passes it to MessageService.SoftDeleteAsync, which enforces author-or-moderator (MessageService.cs:144) and returns an Authorized flag the controller maps to Forbid() (MessagesController.cs:126). The admin-only role-mutation endpoint is constrained to grant/revoke only the moderator role (ModerationController.cs:237) so the API cannot escalate to admin.

Ban-check asymmetry on write endpoints: PostMessage, Upvote, and Report all guard with `if (user.IsBanned) return Forbid()` (MessagesController.cs:73,95,138), but RemoveUpvote (line 105-112) and SoftDelete (line 117-129) deliberately omit the ban check. This is defensible (a banned user retracting a vote or deleting their own content is harmless), but a reviewer must reason about each endpoint individually rather than trusting one uniform rule.

ChatHub (Realtime/ChatHub.cs) has no [Authorize] attribute, so any client can JoinRoom/JoinThread over SignalR. The hub only manages presence and broadcasts content-free 'changed' pings (RealtimeNotifier sends no message bodies); clients re-fetch via REST. Since room reads are already public, this matches the public-read trust boundary rather than leaking protected data, but the JWT-from-query wiring (Program.cs:178-184) exists without the hub actually requiring auth.

No audit logging on privileged moderation/admin state changes: Ban/Unban (ModerationController.cs:205-231), UpdateRole (233-248), and report Resolve/Dismiss (197-203) mutate users and reports with no audit trail of who acted. Not a bypass, but privileged actions are untraceable.
authorization_consistency.issues_preventing_higher_score: Ban-check asymmetry across sibling write endpoints (RemoveUpvote/SoftDelete omit the IsBanned guard that PostMessage/Upvote/Report apply) forces per-endpoint reasoning; ChatHub lacks [Authorize] despite JWT-from-query wiring; and privileged moderation actions (ban/role/report mutations) have no audit logging.

evidence: Codebase comment ratio is 0.022 (very low); every comment sampled explains WHY, not WHAT — e.g. MessageService.cs:64-65 explains reparenting to bound tree depth, TokenService.cs:46-60 explains replay-detection and atomic revoke, ModerationController.cs:215 explains why a ban revokes refresh tokens.

Only 10 XML <summary> docs in the entire backend, all on genuinely non-obvious shared logic (HighlightsService single-flight cache, MessageReader.ResolveRootAsync cycle guarding, MessageEnricher's no-N+1 batch loading). No docstring bloat on trivial 2-line methods.

No nosy debug logging or object dumps. Logging is sparse and purposeful: LogWarning on email/SMTP failures (AuthController.cs:77, SmtpEmailSender.cs:19), LogInformation on cleanup pass results (RefreshTokenCleanupService.cs:42). Two frontend console.debug calls (App.tsx:37,168) are at non-critical failure boundaries with WHY comments above them.

No defensive overengineering: try-catch blocks wrap real I/O only — image storage (MessageService.cs:40-48), clipboard/localStorage (appSupport.ts:50-55,64-89,106-111), SMTP. Null checks guard genuine API boundaries (user input, DB lookups), not non-nullable typed values.

No pass-through wrappers: api.ts requestJson/requestNoContent add 204 handling and JSON parsing; dispatch/send add the refresh-and-retry; ModerationController Resolve/Dismiss parameterize SetReportStatus with distinct enum values. All add real behavior.
Domain-precise naming throughout (EnrichAsync, RotateRefreshTokenAsync, ResolveRootAsync, SoftDeleteAsync); no generic handleData/processItem/doOperation in domain code.
No placeholder/boilerplate residue: zero TODO/FIXME/HACK markers and no NotImplementedException across production source. 'placeholder=' hits are all legitimate JSX input placeholders.
