cross_module_architecture.dimension_character: This is a small, deliberately layered codebase whose dependency direction is coherent and intentional end to end: the backend flows downward from controllers through feature services to a pure Domain leaf with no reciprocal directory edges, and the frontend is a strictly acyclic graph rooted at a single App.tsx composition root. Cross-module reuse (the shared Score formula, HighlightsCache key, RealtimeNotifier) is centralized in the correct owners rather than duplicated, and the cross-feature edge from Moderation into Messages' highlights is an intentional, downward reuse rather than a cycle. The only friction is cosmetic — the Domain hub folder blends entities with static text helpers — which does not create blast radius or boundary ambiguity for a new developer.
cross_module_architecture.score_rationale: A developer here can trust dependency direction and boundaries immediately, which is the hallmark of the 90+ band; there are no recurring boundary violations, cycles, hub modules with surprising ripple, import-time side effects, or compatibility shims. It falls short of exemplary only because the Domain hub mixes entity and utility roles, a minor single-folder blur, so I place it at 93.0 rather than higher. The lone mechanical near-duplicate signal on MessageEnricher.cs is a false positive (the detector matched the class name against its single same-file method), so it does not pull the score down.

high_level_elegance.dimension_character: The codebase has genuinely strong, domain-aligned decomposition: backend feature folders and cross-cutting concerns (Domain/Data/Auth/Realtime/Storage/Email) map cleanly to capabilities, a small pattern set (thin controller -> feature service, result-object domain errors, CQRS-style Messages split) is applied consistently, and the frontend mirrors the same view/component/support discipline. The defects are isolated and cosmetic rather than structural: ModerationController skips the controller/service split its siblings use, an empty leftover Controllers/ directory lingers, and the otherwise-excellent codebase_analysis.md documents a frontend file layout (monolithic views.tsx/ui.tsx) that no longer exists. A new engineer could confidently explain why nearly every package exists and where to put new code.
high_level_elegance.score_rationale: This sits in the strong band (around 90): ownership and change boundaries are predictable and the architectural pattern set is small and consistent, which a developer can trust. It is held just below 90 by uneven layering in one feature (Moderation has no service while Messages/Rooms do), a scaffolding straggler (empty Controllers/), and a reference doc whose structure section has drifted from reality — recurring-but-minor friction rather than the systemic decomposition problems that define the 80 band.

convention_outlier.dimension_character: Conventions are strong and predictable across both stacks: the C# backend is rigorously uniform in controller shape, naming, DTO records, namespace-to-folder mapping, and Result-type protocols, and the TypeScript frontend consistently uses camelCase module files with named-only exports and thematic component grouping. The single genuine outlier is cosmetic and isolated: views/feeds.tsx breaks the PascalCase casing used by its three sibling view files. Structural differences between feature folders (Moderation lacking a service layer) are reasonable variation rather than convention drift.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: A developer can learn a pattern in one area and trust it holds nearly everywhere, which fits the 90 band (strong, only an isolated rough edge). The score is held just under 90 by the views/ casing island, which is real and visible in the import block but trivial in scope and impact; no pervasive naming drift, export-pattern mixing, or behavioral-protocol divergence exists to pull it lower.

error_consistency.dimension_character: Error handling here is unusually disciplined and layered: services return typed Result records or null for expected domain outcomes, the Storage layer throws a typed ImageStorageException that the service catches and converts at a clean boundary, and controllers funnel everything into one `{ error = "CODE" }` HTTP envelope with stable SCREAMING_SNAKE codes mirrored on the frontend's single ApiError type. Every throw and catch in the codebase is intentional and either preserves the original exception (`throw;`), translates it to a domain code, or is a documented best-effort/top-level boundary (mail send, background cleanup, startup fail-fast). A developer can confidently predict how any failure propagates from service to controller to client.
error_consistency.score_rationale: The per-directory 'mixed strategy' signal is the intentional layer separation the rubric explicitly tells reviewers to skip (Result in services, throw in Storage/startup), so it does not pull the score down; what I observed is a coherent, context-preserving model with no silent swallowing and no bare-string error types. This is well above the 80 'solid but uneven' anchor and near the 90+ 'a developer trusts what they see' band; it falls short of exemplary only because a couple of DB-write I/O boundaries rely on the generic 500 handler rather than a feature-local strategy and the error contracts are documented only as in-code string codes (no ProducesResponseType/OpenAPI enumeration).

naming_quality.dimension_character: Naming is a systemic strength: across a compact 2611-LOC codebase, function, type, and variable names reliably predict behavior, using domain vocabulary (Highlights, Enrich, Cursor pagination, Presence, RefreshToken rotation) consistently in both the C# API and the React/TS SPA. There are effectively no generic-verb names (no Process/Handle/Do/Manage in domain code), no Get* that mutate, and no Is*/Has* that return non-booleans. The only friction is a single slightly-generic private helper (dispatch in api.ts) and a couple of param names that could be sharper — isolated cosmetics against an otherwise precise whole.
naming_quality.score_rationale: A developer can read names here and correctly anticipate what code does without opening implementations, which is the hallmark of the 90+ band; the command/read split, predicate honesty, and consistent casing across layers push it toward exemplary. It falls short of 100 only because of trivial, isolated rough edges (the generic dispatch helper, messageId-named thread ids) rather than any recurring or misleading pattern, landing solidly in the strong range.

abstraction_fitness.dimension_character: Abstractions in this codebase are uniformly earned: the only two interfaces wrap genuine external boundaries (SMTP, filesystem) with concrete test-seam and volatility rationale, and every service/reader/enricher carries real validation, projection, caching, or security policy rather than forwarding calls. The two-tier feature shape (thin controller that translates domain Result records to HTTP -> cohesive service) keeps indirection cheap and honest, with no delegate-only layers, no cross-layer manager chains, and no broadly-plumbed option bags. The only blemish is a single trivial one-line wrapper (NormalizeSlug -> Slug.Normalize).
abstraction_fitness.score_rationale: This sits in the strong band (90+): a developer benefits from essentially every layer of indirection, with interface honesty and direct definitions throughout, anchoring near the 'generally strong with a few layers that feel overbuilt but don't slow the developer down' description. It is held just under exemplary by one trivial pass-through helper and by the fact that the abstraction surface is so thin there is little demonstrated substitution strategy beyond the two boundary adapters, so the score lands at 92.5 rather than higher.

dependency_health.dimension_character: The dependency set is lean, cohesive, and entirely purposeful: 5 frontend production deps and 8 backend packages, with every single one verifiably exercised in the code and no overlapping-purpose libraries (one HTTP layer via native fetch, one charting library, no redundant date/util libs). Build-only tooling like the EF Core Design package is correctly scoped with PrivateAssets, and the lock file resolves cleanly to single versions. The only blemish is cosmetic patch-version drift within the backend's ASP.NET Core 10.0.x package family.
dependency_health.score_rationale: This sits near the 100 exemplary anchor: zero unused dependencies, zero duplicate-purpose libraries, no heavy-dep-for-light-use, and a clean lock file all point to a dependency set a developer can trust. It falls just short of perfect because the backend pins a few packages at differing patch levels (10.0.0/10.0.2/10.0.7/10.0.8) on the same major line, a minor consistency nit that does not amount to a real conflict.

low_level_elegance.dimension_character: Function and class internals across this codebase are consistently direct and proportionate: guard clauses and early returns make happy paths immediately visible, state mutation is explicit and bounded (locks in PresenceTracker, atomic DB updates in TokenService, temp-file+move in image storage), and edge-case handling is precise without defensive sprawl — try/catch appears only at genuine I/O boundaries. Helper extraction is balanced and stylistically uniform across MessageReader/MessageService/HighlightsService/RoomService, avoiding both monoliths and micro-fragmentation, and the same discipline carries into the frontend hooks and realtime teardown. The only blemish is one redundant branch construct in ModerationController.TryParseStatus and a little inline-expression repetition in a React tree component.
low_level_elegance.score_rationale: The dimension is systemically strong: a developer reading any individual function can trace it without surprises, and the craft (cycle guards, single-flight caching, magic-byte validation, disposal guards) reflects genuine attention to local correctness — well into the 'strong' (90) band. It falls short of exemplary because of one concrete redundant-branch defect (TryParseStatus re-deriving its result instead of using the computed switch) plus trivial inline repetition, isolated rough edges that pull it to 91.5 rather than the 80-band reserved for codebases with recurring local complexity.

mid_level_elegance.dimension_character: Handoffs across this codebase's layers are deliberate and consistent: services expose explicit result records, controllers translate them to HTTP in one obvious place, error propagation flows in a single predictable direction (storage throws a coded domain exception -> service folds it into a result -> controller maps it to status), and JSON/auth/realtime translation each live in a single centralized seam. The frictions are minor and isolated — error discriminators are bare strings rather than enums, one notifier overload is dead, and a scoring rule is duplicated across the C#/TS boundary — none of which obscure the orchestration. A developer making a cross-module change would find the contracts explicit and the seams easy to trace.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: This sits at the strong end of the scale (90 = trustworthy with only isolated rough edges): the seam patterns are systemic, the orchestration reads as composition rather than tangled back-and-forth, and translation happens in one obvious place per boundary. It falls short of the 90s because the service->controller error contract relies on literal string matching, a dead notifier overload lingers, and the highlights recency formula is silently duplicated across the language boundary — recurring-but-small frictions that keep it just below 'no material issues.'

test_strategy.dimension_character: The server test strategy is genuinely strong and well-architected: a clear pyramid of fast pure-logic unit tests plus full-stack integration tests against a real throwaway Postgres, covering every critical backend path (auth token lifecycle and theft detection, RBAC, message/thread/vote/report flows, cursor pagination, moderation, realtime, image validation) by asserting contracts rather than internals, with no snapshot overuse and isolated per-class containers. The defects are bounded and at the edges — the React/TS frontend has no tests whatsoever, and one integration test leans on a 5ms delay for ordering — so a developer changing server code would feel well-protected, while a developer changing frontend code has no safety net. Overall the strengths are systemic and the gaps are localized.
test_strategy.score_rationale: This lands solidly in the strong band (90 = trustworthy with isolated rough edges) for the server, pulled toward 87 by the complete absence of frontend tests in a two-tier app and a minor timing reliance. It is well above 80 (solid-but-uneven) because the server coverage is broad, behavior-focused, low-coupling, and uses real infrastructure rather than fragile mocks; it falls short of the low-90s because one full client layer is unvalidated, which a careful reviewer would flag as a real strategic blind spot.

api_surface_coherence.dimension_character: The public API surface is strongly coherent and predictable: controllers share one shape (DI primary constructors, ActionResult<T>, trailing CancellationToken, rate-limit partitions), services return a consistent family of sealed result records that controllers translate to HTTP codes uniformly, and pagination/DTO conventions hold across modules. The only real friction is a dead RealtimeNotifier.MessageChangedAsync overload with no callers and a wide 8-parameter Score overload; both are isolated rough edges rather than systemic inconsistency.
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: A developer can reliably predict endpoint and service shapes from a single example, which places this near the 90 'strong' anchor. It falls short of exemplary because of one genuinely dead overload that muddies the notifier's surface and a primitive-heavy 8-param scoring overload, but these are local and do not force the developer to read each endpoint individually.

authorization_consistency.dimension_character: Authorization is applied with strong, deliberate consistency: role strings are fully centralized in a single Roles constant set and used uniformly, [Authorize] gates every mutating endpoint while public reads are an intentional forum design, and ban enforcement is layered across token mint, refresh rotation, and write controllers with session revocation on ban. The few rough edges are minor and mostly intentional: a ban-check asymmetry where RemoveUpvote/SoftDelete omit the IsBanned guard that their write-endpoint siblings apply, an unauthenticated SignalR hub that only carries presence/pings (consistent with the public-read boundary), and the absence of audit logging on privileged moderation actions. A developer can largely trust that auth patterns hold across protected resources.
authorization_consistency.score_rationale: This sits in the strong band (90): centralized permission constants, uniform [Authorize] coverage on writes, and multi-layer ban enforcement give a developer reliable, predictable auth patterns with no magic literals or missing guards on protected mutations. It falls short of exemplary because the per-endpoint ban-check asymmetry requires case-by-case reasoning, the ChatHub carries JWT-from-query wiring yet does not require auth, and privileged admin/moderation actions lack audit trails — friction points rather than coverage gaps.

ai_generated_debt.dimension_character: This codebase reads as deliberately hand-crafted with respect to AI-generated debt: comments are rare (0.022 ratio) and uniformly explain non-obvious business rules and concurrency invariants rather than restating code, logging is sparse and purposeful, and there is no defensive sprawl, docstring bloat, pass-through indirection, or placeholder residue. Helpers consistently add real behavior (retry, parsing, parameterization, single-flight caching) and naming is domain-precise everywhere. A developer's attention lands squarely on the logic, never on ceremony.
ai_generated_debt.score_rationale: Against the global anchors this sits at the exemplary end (100 = attention spent on logic not noise): no restating comments, no nosy logging, no defensive overengineering, no boilerplate copy-paste, and no TODO/placeholder leftovers were found anywhere in production code. It falls just short of a perfect score only because a few methods carry wide parameter lists and a per-row loop that are ordinary craft nits, not generated-style noise, so the dimension is rated 95.5.

incomplete_migration.dimension_character: This codebase shows essentially no incomplete-migration drift: a single HTTP client (fetch), single realtime transport (SignalR), all-functional all-TypeScript frontend, no deprecated/Obsolete symbols, no sync-over-async shims, and no V1/V2 dual code paths. The one prior architecture (Supabase RLS) survives only as a test-parity reference, meaning the port to C# authorization is complete; the only migration-era runtime residue is a consistently-applied RootMessageId==Guid.Empty backfill convention for legacy thread rows.
incomplete_migration.score_rationale: A developer can trust which pattern to follow everywhere — there is no old/new coexistence creating ambiguity, which sits at the top of the scale (90+ = mostly complete with at most minor, non-confusing legacy residue). The score is held just below exemplary because the Guid.Empty legacy-row handling is duplicated across four call sites rather than centralized, a small recognizable bit of migration residue, but it is uniform, documented, and never produces dual-path confusion.

package_organization.dimension_character: Package organization is a clear strength: the backend follows feature/concern folders with namespaces matching directories one-to-one, a pure dependency-free Domain/ core (fan_in 21, fan_out 0), strictly unidirectional cross-directory coupling, and no flat-directory overload. A developer can navigate by structure alone and reliably predict where to find or add code. The only friction is cosmetic — an empty leftover Controllers/ directory whose name contradicts the established vertical-slice convention, and Domain/ co-locating two static text utilities (Slug.cs, Text.cs) with persistence entities.
package_organization.score_rationale: This sits in the strong band (90): directory layout is coherent and predictable with clean boundaries and no coupling-direction failures, so a developer trusts the structure. It falls short of exemplary (95+) only because of two minor placement outliers — the empty, misleadingly-named Controllers/ scaffolding directory and the entity/utility mix in Domain/ — neither of which materially impedes navigation.

initialization_coupling.dimension_character: Initialization is uniformly handled through the ASP.NET Core DI container, so a developer can add or reorder service registrations without worrying about import order or hidden boot-time side effects. There are no module-scope DB/file/network calls, no environment reads at import time in the running app, and the only static state is immutable constants, lookup tables, and a deliberately process-wide synchronization gate; the lone singleton (PresenceTracker) is self-contained and lock-guarded. Startup migration and seeding follow the canonical post-Build() scoped pattern with explicit error logging.
initialization_coupling.score_rationale: This sits near the top anchor (100 = a developer can import any module without worrying about boot-order or side effects): wiring is framework-owned, config is runtime-resolved via IOptions/IConfiguration, and there is no fragile global ordering. It falls just short of exemplary only because a Scoped HighlightsService deliberately relies on a process-wide static SemaphoreSlim plus singleton IMemoryCache to coordinate a shared snapshot, a correct but implicitly-global piece of coordination a reader must recognize; no defects warrant a finding.

design_coherence.dimension_character: Design decisions are notably consistent: a single per-feature controller/service/reader/DTO shape, a uniform Result-record command idiom, and a repeated batch-load-then-GetValueOrDefault enrichment recipe mean a developer who learns one feature can predict every other. Functions are focused with earned helpers, recursion appears only where the reply tree demands it, and the frontend mirrors this with a one-line-per-endpoint api layer and data-driven moderation UI. The only friction is a localized one: MessagesController maps service errors through magic string-literal branches rather than the structured result shape its siblings use.
design_coherence.score_rationale: This sits in the strong band (90): patterns are systemic and uniform across both backend and frontend, the flagged duplication is a false positive, and no function mixes unrelated responsibilities. It is held just under exemplary by the string-literal error-mapping wrinkle in MessagesController.CreateMessageAsync, which is the single place the consistent command-result idiom frays — minor and isolated, not a structural drag.

contract_coherence.dimension_character: Contract coherence is a systemic strength: REST DTOs are record types that mirror the frontend TS shapes field-for-field under a single global snake_case policy, return types honestly cover all paths via ActionResult<T> and discriminated Result records, and no getter name hides a mutation. The one crack is the SignalR boundary, where AddSignalR() is left unconfigured so hub payloads serialize in camelCase while the identical DTO over REST is snake_case, forcing the frontend to defensively read both casings for the presence event.
contract_coherence.score_rationale: The REST/DTO surface is exemplary and reinforced by parity tests, which would place it in the low-to-mid 90s; the isolated but real SignalR casing incoherence (one unconfigured serializer producing a dual wire shape, with the consumer working around it) pulls it off a 90+ 'trust everything' rating into the 'strong with an isolated rough edge' band. This is a single-edit fix (AddSignalR().AddJsonProtocol with the snake_case policy) affecting one boundary, not a pervasive contract problem.

logic_clarity.dimension_character: Logic is consistently direct and intention-revealing across the most complex areas: cycle-guarded parent/tree walks (ResolveRootAsync, LoadStatsAsync, BuildReplyNodes), single-flight cache recompute with double-check, atomic refresh-token rotation gated on affected-row counts, and precise streaming/header validation. Branches are necessary and distinct, early returns keep happy paths visible, and there are no dead paths, identical branches, always-true conditions, or boolean expressions that collapse to a literal. The lone blemish is one helper (TryParseStatus) that re-derives its return value instead of using the switch it already evaluated.
logic_clarity.score_rationale: Control flow can be traced without surprises throughout both the C# backend and the React/TS frontend, with the logic-dense ranking and thread-tree code being the cleanest parts — well above the 80 anchor's 'regular redundant branches/dead paths'. It falls short of 100 because of the single TryParseStatus redundancy (a localized, cosmetic double-evaluation), which is the only avoidable complexity found and keeps this in the strong-but-not-exemplary range.

type_safety.dimension_character: Type safety is a systemic strength: nullable reference types and TS strict mode are both enabled and genuinely respected, with nullable return types that honestly model not-found paths, DTOs whose nullability mirrors the domain, and every null-suppression operator (`!`, `null!`) either idiomatic EF boilerplate or correctly guarded by a preceding null filter. The frontend is free of `any` and uses only idiomatic casts. The only blemishes are a handful of redundant non-null assertions in feeds.tsx and a slightly awkward string-to-enum re-parse at the moderation query boundary — both cosmetic, not unsound.
type_safety.score_rationale: Annotations can be trusted as accurate documentation of runtime behavior throughout, which places this well into the strong band (90+). It falls short of exemplary because a developer would occasionally hit a redundant `!` that flow analysis already disproves (feeds.tsx:192/209) and a boundary enum re-parse that bypasses model binding — minor, isolated rough edges rather than recurring friction, so the score sits just above the 90 anchor.

issues.summary: ModerationController holds all analytics/aggregation logic inline while sibling features use a controller+service split
issues.suggestion: Extract the read/aggregation logic (analytics windowing, top-rooms/top-posts/trend queries) into a ModerationAnalyticsService (or ModerationReader) registered in DI, mirroring MessageReader/HighlightsService, leaving the controller to translate requests and results. Keep it a single straightforward service; do not over-fragment.
evidence: ModerationController.cs is 379 LOC and contains private aggregation helpers LoadTrendAsync, LoadTopRoomsAsync, LoadTopPostsAsync, CountRoleMembersAsync, plus direct AppDbContext/UserManager access for analytics, report triage, bans, and role grants.

By contrast MessagesController delegates to MessageService/MessageReader and RoomsController delegates to RoomService, so Moderation is the only feature whose controller owns substantial domain logic — a developer expecting the established per-feature service split will be surprised here.

issues.summary: codebase_analysis.md Directory Structure and Gaps sections no longer match runtime reality
issues.suggestion: Update the Directory Structure section to reflect frontend/src/views/ and frontend/src/components/, refresh the line counts, and prune the now-resolved Gaps items (git, Dockerfile, README path) so the document remains a trustworthy reference for the structure.
evidence: Doc documents the frontend as single files views.tsx (861 lines) and ui.tsx (532 lines), but those files do not exist; the code is split into views/ (Auth, Moderation, Profile, feeds) and components/ (chrome, messages, overlays).

Doc lists App.tsx as 536 lines (actual 589) and its Gaps section still asserts 'Not a git repository', 'No Dockerfiles for the API', and a README referencing a stale alb_sheshi/ path — all contradicted by the current tree (a .git dir, server/Dockerfile, and a server/README.md that points at server/Sheshi.Api).

issues.summary: Empty leftover Controllers/ directory implies a flat-controllers layout the codebase abandoned for feature folders
issues.suggestion: Delete the empty server/Sheshi.Api/Controllers directory so the feature-foldered layout is the single, unambiguous controller organization.
evidence: server/Sheshi.Api/Controllers/ exists but contains no files; all controllers actually live under Features/*, Auth/, and Realtime/, so the directory is a scaffolding remnant that briefly suggests a second, unused organizational convention.

issues.summary: views/feeds.tsx is lowercase while sibling view files Auth.tsx/Moderation.tsx/Profile.tsx are PascalCase
issues.suggestion: Rename feeds.tsx to Feeds.tsx (and update the import at App.tsx:26) so all files in views/ share one PascalCase file-naming convention.
evidence: frontend/src/views/ contains Auth.tsx, Moderation.tsx, Profile.tsx (PascalCase) and feeds.tsx (lowercase).

feeds.tsx exports Home, RoomView, ThreadView — top-level routed views with the same role as the components in the PascalCase view files (App.tsx:26 imports them alongside the others; App.tsx:466-556 routes them identically).
No comment or structural reason differentiates feeds.tsx from its siblings; the casing is the only difference.

issues.reasoning: Self-comparison of a one-method class against its sole method; no real duplication and nothing convention-related to fix.
Detector compared the class declaration line against its only method body, yielding a 97% match; there is no real duplicated code to extract.

issues.summary: Backend ASP.NET Core 10.0.x packages pinned at inconsistent patch levels (10.0.0/10.0.2/10.0.7/10.0.8)
issues.suggestion: Bump the Microsoft.AspNetCore.OpenApi reference (and other Microsoft.AspNetCore.* entries) to the same 10.0.8 patch where available so the in-house Microsoft package family pins uniformly; leave third-party packages (Apple OAuth, Npgsql) at their own latest patch.
evidence: AspNet.Security.OAuth.Apple is 10.0.0, Microsoft.AspNetCore.OpenApi is 10.0.7, Npgsql.EntityFrameworkCore.PostgreSQL is 10.0.2, while the Microsoft.AspNetCore.* auth/identity packages are 10.0.8.
Not a true version conflict (different vendors version independently), but the spread is easy to drift further and harder to audit at a glance.

issues.summary: TryParseStatus computes parsed via switch then re-derives success from a parallel three-literal check, evaluating ToLowerInvariant three times
issues.suggestion: Make the switch the single source of truth: switch to `string?` or use a value-tuple, e.g. `return (parsed, ok) = status.ToLowerInvariant() switch { "open" => (ReportStatus.Open, true), ... , _ => (default, false) }` — or simply compute `var ok = ...switch{...=> true, _ => false}` alongside `parsed` so ToLowerInvariant runs once and the branch set is not duplicated.
evidence: Lines 290-300: the switch on status.ToLowerInvariant() sets `parsed`, then `return status.ToLowerInvariant() is "open" or "resolved" or "dismissed";` re-lowercases and re-lists the same three literals, ignoring the switch result.

The `_ => default` arm makes the out value indistinguishable for an invalid input (default == ReportStatus.Open), so the separate boolean check exists only to compensate for the discarded switch outcome.

issues.summary: Service result error codes cross the service->controller seam as bare strings matched by literal comparison
issues.suggestion: Replace the free-string Error field on the result records with a small enum (e.g. MessageCreateError { RoomNotFound, ParentNotFound, ParentRoomMismatch, ... }) so the producer and the controller's switch share a compiler-checked discriminator; keep the HTTP-status mapping in the controller as-is. This adds no indirection — it swaps a stringly-typed field for an enum field on the same record.
evidence: MessageService.CreateMessageAsync returns MessageCreateResult.Failed("ROOM_NOT_FOUND" / "PARENT_NOT_FOUND" / "PARENT_ROOM_MISMATCH"); the controller re-discriminates with result.Error == "ROOM_NOT_FOUND" etc. (MessagesController.cs lines 80-85).

RoomsController.Create switches on the literal "ROOM_EXISTS" string (lines 33-38). The producer and consumer share an implicit string contract with no compiler enforcement, so a code rename or typo on one side silently degrades to the generic BadRequest branch.

issues.summary: RealtimeNotifier.MessageChangedAsync(Guid, Guid?, ...) overload has no callers
issues.suggestion: Delete the unused MessageChangedAsync(Guid roomId, Guid? threadId = null, ...) overload so the realtime seam exposes exactly the one notification shape callers actually use.
evidence: RealtimeNotifier exposes two MessageChangedAsync overloads (lines 7 and 14). A grep of the API project shows all four call sites in MessageService use the MessageChangeDto overload; the (Guid roomId, Guid? threadId) overload is unreferenced.
A vestigial overload on the realtime seam widens the boundary surface and invites future callers to use the lower-fidelity notification path (no message_changed payload).

issues.summary: Frontend hotScore duplicates backend HighlightsService recency constants across the C#/TS boundary
issues.suggestion: Add a short comment on both formulas cross-referencing each other (e.g. '// keep in sync with HighlightsService.Score recency decay') so the deliberate duplication across the language boundary is visible to whoever edits either side. Do not extract a shared abstraction — the runtimes cannot share code and the client copy is intentionally a lightweight approximation.
evidence: appSupport.ts hotScore uses 42 / pow(ageHours+1, 0.95) + 14 / pow(ageHours+1, 0.45) plus reply_count*30 and upvotes*12 (lines 191-198), the same magic constants as HighlightsService.Score (lines 84-89).
The two implementations are unlinked; a tweak to the server ranking formula would silently desync from the client-side 'hot' re-sort with nothing flagging the drift.

issues.summary: React/TS frontend SPA has no automated tests and no configured test runner
issues.suggestion: Add a lightweight Vitest setup and cover the highest-value pure modules first: api.ts request/response mapping, roles.ts gating, and realtime.ts connection/event handling with a mocked hub. This validates the client contract without needing a full browser harness.
evidence: No *.test.* or *.spec.* files exist under frontend/src; package.json scripts contain only dev/build/preview — no test runner (vitest/jest) is wired up.

frontend/src contains the API client (api.ts), SignalR realtime layer (realtime.ts), role logic (roles.ts), hooks (appHooks.ts), and feature views/components — none are exercised by any test, so client-side regressions (request shaping, optimistic vote/report handling, realtime reconnection, role gating) are caught only manually.

issues.summary: Pagination ordering test depends on a 5ms Task.Delay to separate CreatedAt timestamps
issues.suggestion: Remove the Task.Delay and make ordering deterministic by asserting against the index's full ordering (CreatedAt desc, Id desc) or by controlling CreatedAt directly so the test does not depend on timing resolution.
evidence: Threads_store_root_depth_and_feeds_page_by_cursor posts two messages separated by `await Task.Delay(5)` (line 215), then asserts which post appears on page one — correctness hinges on the 5ms gap producing distinct CreatedAt values under the DB/clock resolution.
The keyset index includes Id as a tiebreaker, so the test could be made deterministic without relying on wall-clock spacing.

issues.summary: RealtimeNotifier.MessageChangedAsync(Guid roomId, Guid? threadId, ct) overload has zero callers
issues.suggestion: Delete the unused MessageChangedAsync(Guid roomId, Guid? threadId = null, ct) overload so the notifier exposes a single MessageChangeDto-based method.
evidence: RealtimeNotifier.cs:7 declares MessageChangedAsync(Guid roomId, Guid? threadId = null, CancellationToken ct = default).
All four call sites (MessageService.cs:93, 111, 133, 155) invoke the MessageChangeDto overload (RealtimeNotifier.cs:14); the (Guid roomId, ...) overload is unreferenced across the project and tests.
Two same-named public methods with different parameter shapes, one unused, makes the notifier surface ambiguous about the intended call form.

issues.summary: Sibling write endpoints inconsistently apply the IsBanned guard
issues.suggestion: If the omission is intentional (banned users may retract votes / delete their own content), add a one-line comment on RemoveUpvote and SoftDelete stating why the ban guard is skipped, so the asymmetry is self-documenting rather than something each reviewer must re-derive.
evidence: PostMessage (CreateMessageAsync line 73), Upvote (line 95), and Report (line 138) all enforce `if (user.IsBanned) return Forbid();`.
RemoveUpvote (lines 105-112) and SoftDelete (lines 117-129) deliberately omit the IsBanned check, so the rule a reviewer learns from the other handlers does not hold uniformly across siblings.

issues.summary: ChatHub has no [Authorize] despite JWT-from-query wiring for /hub
issues.suggestion: Either drop the unused access_token-from-query wiring if the hub is meant to be public, or add [Authorize] to ChatHub so the wiring and the trust boundary agree; document the chosen stance so the intent is explicit.
evidence: ChatHub (ChatHub.cs:5) defines JoinRoom/JoinThread with no [Authorize] attribute, so any anonymous client can join any room/thread group.
Program.cs:178-184 wires access_token-from-query handling specifically for /hub, implying tokens are expected, yet the hub never requires authentication.
Mitigating factor: the hub only broadcasts presence counts and content-free 'changed' pings (RealtimeNotifier.cs); no message bodies traverse the hub and room reads are already public.

issues.summary: Privileged moderation/admin actions leave no audit trail
issues.suggestion: Log the acting user id (User.GetUserId()) and target id for ban/unban/role-change/report-resolution via the existing ILogger so privileged actions are traceable; avoid introducing a new audit subsystem unless retention requirements demand it.
evidence: Ban (lines 205-220), Unban (222-231), UpdateRole (233-248), and report Resolve/Dismiss (197-203, SetReportStatus 280-288) mutate user state and report status without recording which moderator/admin performed the action.
The look_for list calls out 'service role / admin bypass without audit logging'; these are not bypasses but they are unauditable privileged operations.

issues.summary: Empty Controllers/ directory contradicts the feature-folder controller convention
issues.suggestion: Delete the empty server/Sheshi.Api/Controllers/ directory. No move/import updates needed since it holds no files; run `dotnet build server/Sheshi.Api` afterward to confirm nothing referenced it.
evidence: server/Sheshi.Api/Controllers/ exists but contains zero files (leftover from `dotnet new webapi` scaffolding).

Controllers actually live in feature folders: Features/Messages/MessagesController.cs, Features/Moderation/ModerationController.cs, Features/Rooms/RoomsController.cs, Auth/AuthController.cs, Auth/MeController.cs, Realtime/PresenceController.cs.
The folder name implies a flat-by-MVC-role placement convention that the codebase deliberately does not use, sending a contradictory navigation signal.

issues.summary: Domain/ co-locates static text utilities (Slug.cs, Text.cs) with persistence entities
issues.suggestion: Optional, low priority: if the team wants stricter cohesion, group the two static text helpers under a Domain/Text/ (or Domain/Normalization/) subfolder — staged as: 1) create Domain/Text/, 2) move Slug.cs and Text.cs in, 3) namespaces can stay Sheshi.Api.Domain so no import changes are required, 4) run `dotnet build`. Given both are tiny and already correctly in Domain by namespace, leaving them in place is also defensible.
evidence: Domain/ contains persistence entities (Message.cs, Room.cs, Vote.cs, Report.cs, RefreshToken.cs, ApplicationUser.cs) plus Enums.cs, alongside two static helper classes Slug.cs (URL slug normalization) and Text.cs (text clipping).
Slug and Text are static utility surfaces, a different concern than the EF-mapped entities the folder otherwise holds, though both are broadly imported (fan_in 21) and operate on domain text values.

issues.summary: SignalR hub payloads use camelCase while REST uses snake_case for the same DTOs; frontend reads both casings defensively
issues.suggestion: Configure SignalR to use the same naming policy as MVC: builder.Services.AddSignalR().AddJsonProtocol(o => o.PayloadSerializerOptions.PropertyNamingPolicy = JsonNamingPolicy.SnakeCaseLower); then drop the dual-casing fallback in App.tsx/realtime.ts so the presence event has one stable snake_case contract.
evidence: Program.cs:57 calls builder.Services.AddSignalR() with no AddJsonProtocol configuration, so SignalR uses its default serializer (camelCase) rather than the MVC SnakeCaseLower policy set at Program.cs:32-34.

ChatHub.JoinRoom/LeaveRoom send PresenceDto(Guid RoomId, int Count) over the 'presence' event, which therefore arrives as {roomId,count}; the same DTO over the REST presence path uses the snake_case pipeline.
frontend/src/App.tsx:292-295 and realtime.ts type RealtimePresenceUpdate read `update.roomId || update.room_id`, defending against both casings instead of relying on one contract.
RealtimeStorageModerationTests asserts the REST /api/rooms/presence shape but does not pin the SignalR 'presence' payload casing, so the ambiguity is untested.

issues.summary: TryParseStatus re-runs the lowercased string-set check for its return instead of using the switch it computed
issues.suggestion: Compute the lowercased value once and let the switch signal validity, e.g. `var normalized = status.ToLowerInvariant(); switch return a value/flag` — set `parsed` and return true for the three cases, return false in the default arm, so the valid set is declared in exactly one place.
evidence: Line 292: `parsed = status.ToLowerInvariant() switch { "open" => ..., "resolved" => ..., "dismissed" => ..., _ => default };` assigns the out parameter.

Line 299: `return status.ToLowerInvariant() is "open" or "resolved" or "dismissed";` re-evaluates ToLowerInvariant() a second/third time and re-lists the same valid values, so the return is not derived from the switch result and the valid-value list is duplicated.

issues.summary: Redundant non-null assertions on props.thread after an early-return guard already narrows it
issues.suggestion: Drop the `!` on feeds.tsx:192 and :209 (use props.thread.root.room_id / props.thread.root.id); TS already proves non-null after the line 182 guard, matching the adjacent unguarded accesses.
evidence: Line 182 guards `if (!props.thread) { return ... }`, narrowing props.thread to non-null for the rest of the component.
Lines 192 and 209 still use `props.thread!.root....` while the immediately adjacent lines 193-195 access `props.thread.root...` without `!` — the assertions are unnecessary and inconsistent.

issues.summary: ReportStatus enum re-parsed from raw query string with an awkward double-lowercase switch and redundant boolean check
issues.suggestion: Bind [FromQuery] ReportStatus? status directly (ASP.NET parses enum names case-insensitively) and treat null/invalid as a BadRequest, or have TryParseStatus compute the lowercased value once and use Enum.TryParse, removing the duplicated literal set and double ToLowerInvariant().
evidence: TryParseStatus (lines 290-300) maps raw strings to ReportStatus via a switch that falls to `default` (=Open) on no match, then re-evaluates `status.ToLowerInvariant() is 'open' or 'resolved' or 'dismissed'` to derive the success flag — computing ToLowerInvariant twice and duplicating the literal set.
The ReportStatus enum exists but the [FromQuery] string parameter bypasses model binding, hand-rolling the enum parse instead.

add.header: Strictly downward layered backend with pure Domain leaf
add.description: Every directory edge flows downward to a fan-out-0 Domain layer with no reciprocal edges, so common changes have a predictable, bounded blast radius and a new developer can trust dependency direction on sight.

add.header: Shared logic centralized in correct owners, not duplicated
add.description: The single HighlightsService.Score formula, HighlightsCache.Key, and RealtimeNotifier are owned by one module and consumed downward (including Moderation reusing the highlights snapshot), avoiding cross-package duplication and missing-boundary debt.

add.header: Acyclic, composition-rooted frontend module graph
add.description: types -> api -> appSupport -> hooks/components/views -> App forms a clean DAG with no view-to-view or base-to-higher back-edges, so frontend changes stay local to their layer.

add.header: Domain hub folder blends entities with static utilities
add.description: Slug.cs and Text.cs sit alongside persistence entities in the most-imported Domain folder; benign at this size but the dependency hub owns two distinct roles rather than one.

add.header: Domain-aligned feature-folder decomposition
add.description: Backend splits cleanly into Domain/Data/Auth/Email/Realtime/Storage cross-cutting folders plus Features/{Messages,Rooms,Moderation}; each top-level package maps to a capability so ownership and where-to-put-new-code are predictable for a new engineer.

add.header: Small consistent pattern set across features
add.description: Thin controllers delegate to feature services and translate result-object records (MessageCreateResult/RoomCreateResult/etc.) into HTTP, and the Messages feature applies a deliberate CQRS-style split (Service=writes, Reader=reads, Enricher=projection, HighlightsService=cached ranking) with role-documenting XML comments. The same patterns recur rather than each area inventing its own.

add.header: Frontend mirrors backend ownership discipline
add.description: frontend/src separates views/ (pages), components/ (reusable UI), and single-responsibility support modules (api, realtime, appHooks, appSupport, roles, types); App.tsx is a large but genuine top-level state/composition root rather than a concern-mixing dumping ground.

add.header: Moderation feature lacks the controller/service split
add.description: ModerationController owns analytics aggregation, triage, and mutations inline (no ModerationService) while Messages/Rooms split controller from service, making the layering pattern uneven across features even though Moderation is admin/read-mostly with a single consumer.

add.header: codebase_analysis.md structure section has drifted
add.description: The otherwise-excellent reference doc still describes a monolithic frontend (views.tsx/ui.tsx) and stale gaps (no git, no Dockerfile, stale README path) that no longer match the actual views/ + components/ split and current project state, slightly weakening the trustworthiness of the structure docs.

add.header: Uniform C# controller and DTO conventions
add.description: All controllers use primary constructors, [ApiController]/[Route]/[EnableRateLimiting], Async-suffixed methods, and folder-mirroring namespaces; DTOs are positional records in per-feature *Dtos.cs files with *Dto/*Request suffixes. A developer can predict the shape of any new endpoint from one example.

add.header: Consistent frontend module and export conventions
add.description: Non-component frontend files use camelCase names with named-only exports (no default exports anywhere) and group components thematically; this makes import sites and module roles predictable across the SPA.

add.header: views/ filename casing island (feeds.tsx)
add.description: feeds.tsx is lowercase while its sibling routed-view files (Auth.tsx, Moderation.tsx, Profile.tsx) are PascalCase, with no differentiating rationale — a minor, isolated style outlier visible in the App.tsx import block.

add.header: Moderation feature omits a service layer by design
add.description: Messages and Rooms split controller+service, but read-heavy Moderation keeps analytics logic in its controller; this is reasonable per-feature variation, not convention drift, and should not be forced into a unifying abstraction.

add.header: Layered Result + typed-exception error model
add.description: Services return typed Result records (Created/Failed/NotFound/Forbidden factories) or null for expected outcomes while the Storage layer throws a typed ImageStorageException carrying a code; the service catches it and converts to Result at the boundary. The strategy varies by layer on purpose, giving predictable, context-preserving propagation.

add.header: Single HTTP error envelope with stable codes
add.description: Controllers translate every Result/null/exception into one `{ error = "CODE" }` JSON shape using SCREAMING_SNAKE codes, and the frontend's single ApiError type (status + code) parses that envelope at one send() boundary. Failure shape is uniform end to end.

add.header: Only intentional swallows; all top-level/best-effort
add.description: Every catch in the codebase is deliberate: temp-file cleanup with re-throw in image storage, log-and-retry in the background cleanup service, documented best-effort mail send, fail-fast startup rethrow, and frontend localStorage/clipboard fallbacks. No silent swallowing of unexpected failures.

add.header: DB-write boundaries lean on the generic 500 handler
add.description: Token issuance, report, and vote SaveChanges paths have no feature-local transient-failure strategy and rely on app.UseExceptionHandler's INTERNAL_ERROR, and public error contracts exist only as in-code string codes (no ProducesResponseType/OpenAPI enumeration). Consistent with the deliberate fail-to-500 posture but the only soft spot in an otherwise exemplary model.

add.header: Read/write split encoded in class names
add.description: MessageReader (queries) vs MessageService (commands) and HighlightsService/MessageEnricher make responsibility predictable from the type name alone, so a developer knows where a behavior lives without reading bodies.

add.header: Predicate and getter names are behaviorally honest
add.description: Every Is*/Has*/Can* (IsBanned, IsActive, HasValidSignature, canModerate) returns a bool and no Get* mutates state, so names can be trusted as contracts across both C# and TS.

add.header: Domain-aligned verbs, no generic-verb noise
add.description: Action methods use specific domain verbs (RotateRefreshTokenAsync, EnrichAsync, ResolveRootAsync, buildShareTarget, requireAuth); there are no Process/Handle/Do/Manage placeholders in domain code.

add.header: Intentional localized route vocabulary
add.description: Route literals dhoma/tema/profili/moderim are deliberate Albanian URL segments, not naming drift; treat as a convention rather than an outlier.

add.header: Interfaces reserved for real external boundaries
add.description: The only two I* interfaces (IImageStorage, IEmailSender) wrap volatile external resources (filesystem, SMTP) and are substituted in tests; internal services are registered as concrete types, avoiding ceremonial single-impl interfaces.

add.header: Thin controllers translate domain results to HTTP
add.description: Controllers add value by mapping service Result records to status codes and enforcing auth/role/ban policy rather than delegating; no controller-service-manager-repository pass-through chains exist.

add.header: Read/write split into cohesive collaborators
add.description: MessageService (commands), MessageReader (queries), MessageEnricher (projection/N+1 avoidance), and HighlightsService (cached scoring) each hold a distinct, documented responsibility, so layers pay for themselves with real behavior.

add.header: EF/LINQ fluent chains inflate indirection metrics
add.description: Mechanical indirection_hotspots count LINQ query-builder chains, not abstraction layers; these should not be read as delegate stacking.

add.header: Lean fully-utilized dependency set
add.description: All 5 frontend and 8 backend production dependencies are verifiably used in code with no unused or duplicate-purpose libraries, keeping the supply-chain surface minimal and auditable.

add.header: Single library per concern
add.description: One HTTP layer (native fetch in api.ts, no axios), one charting library (chart.js), and no date/util libraries means a developer never has to pick between competing tools for the same job.

add.header: Build-only tooling correctly scoped
add.description: Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.Design uses PrivateAssets=all and build-only IncludeAssets so the design-time package does not leak into runtime, showing deliberate dependency scoping.

add.header: Backend patch-version drift on 10.0.x line
add.description: Backend ASP.NET Core packages mix 10.0.0/10.0.2/10.0.7/10.0.8 pins; harmless (separately versioned vendors) but a minor consistency/audit nit on the in-house Microsoft package family.

add.header: Guard-clause + early-return happy path everywhere
add.description: Services and controllers (MessageService, MessageReader, TokenService, AuthController, MessagesController) uniformly validate with early returns so the success path is linear and visible, and try/catch appears only at real I/O boundaries (image storage, email send). This keeps individual function internals direct and intention-revealing.

add.header: Bounded, explicit state mutation with cycle guards
add.description: Local mutation is consistently bounded: PresenceTracker mutates only under one lock with uniform empty-cleanup, TokenService uses atomic row-count-checked DB updates, and tree walks (ResolveRootAsync, HighlightsService.LoadStatsAsync) carry HashSet seen-set guards against cycles. Side effects are local and proportionate, not sprawling.

add.header: Balanced helper extraction, no micro-fragmentation
add.description: Read/command splits and private helpers (NormalizeLimit, EncodeCursor, EffectiveRootId, ToDtosAsync) are sized to one clear job and the style is consistent across sibling feature modules, avoiding both god-functions and over-extracted one-liners.

add.header: MessageEnricher near-dupe flag is a detector artifact
add.description: The mechanical 'near-duplicate' on MessageEnricher.cs matches the class name against its single EnrichAsync method name (97%); the file contains one cohesive batch-loading projection with no internal duplication. Not a low_level_elegance defect.

add.header: Result-record seam with single-point HTTP translation
add.description: Services return small result records (MessageCreateResult, DeleteMessageResult, CreateRoomResult) carrying entity/dto/error, and each controller maps the error in one obvious switch/if-chain. Inputs and outputs across the service boundary are explicit and minimal, and translation lives in exactly one place per endpoint.

add.header: Coded-exception error propagation is one-directional and predictable
add.description: Storage throws ImageStorageException(code) -> MessageService catches and folds the code into a result -> controller maps to HTTP. Error and lifecycle propagation across the storage/service/controller seam follows one predictable pattern rather than ad-hoc try/catch sprawl.

add.header: Centralized JSON and HTTP-client seams
add.description: Backend snake_case wire contract is configured once in Program.cs (naming policy + enum converter); frontend funnels every call through one send()/ApiError seam with transparent refresh-retry. The cross-runtime contract has a single source of truth on each side, so boundary translation is not scattered.

add.header: Service-to-controller error codes are stringly typed
add.description: Result records expose a free-string Error field that controllers re-discriminate by literal comparison (== "ROOM_NOT_FOUND"). The producer/consumer share an implicit, compiler-unchecked string contract at the seam; consistent today but fragile to renames.

add.header: Real-Postgres integration suite via Testcontainers + WAF
add.description: Integration tests boot the full app (migrations + seeding) against a throwaway Postgres container instead of mocking the data layer, so they validate true runtime behavior and stay resilient to internal refactors. This is the primary reason backend confidence is high.

add.header: Behavior/contract assertions over implementation coupling
add.description: Tests assert HTTP status codes and JSON DTO shapes (locally redeclared records with [JsonPropertyName]) plus observable invariants, not internal call sequences, keeping test-production coupling low. There is zero snapshot/Verify usage.

add.header: Balanced unit layer for pure logic
add.description: UnitTests.cs isolates pure, dependency-free logic (slug/text normalization, highlight scoring, presence tracking, claim parsing, image storage validation) with Theory-driven edge cases, complementing the slower integration suite rather than duplicating it.

add.header: Frontend SPA has no automated tests
add.description: The React/TS client (api client, realtime, hooks, views) has no test files and no configured runner, so client-side regressions are caught only manually — the main strategic blind spot in an otherwise strong portfolio.

add.header: Per-class isolated containers with GUID-based data isolation
add.description: Each test class owns its own ApiFactory/container and isolates data with GUID-unique emails/labels rather than shared mutable global state, with the one shared-fixture caveat documented inline — a managed tradeoff, not fragile cross-test coupling.

add.header: Uniform controller shape across all features
add.description: Every controller uses primary-constructor DI, [ApiController]+[Route("api/...")], ActionResult<T> returns, a trailing CancellationToken, and read/write/auth rate-limit partitions, so a developer can predict any new endpoint's shape from one example.

add.header: Consistent sealed-result error contracts in services
add.description: Command services return small sealed result records (MessageCreateResult, DeleteMessageResult, ReportMessageResult, CreateRoomResult) with Created/Failed/NotFound factories that controllers translate to HTTP codes the same way; reads return nullable DTOs mapped to NotFound(). The failure surface is uniform and self-documenting.

add.header: Dead overload on RealtimeNotifier surface
add.description: MessageChangedAsync(Guid roomId, Guid? threadId, ct) has no callers while the MessageChangeDto overload is used everywhere, leaving an ambiguous unused second shape on the notifier's public API.

add.header: Role strings centralized in Roles constants
add.description: All authorization checks reference the Roles static class (Domain/Enums.cs) — [Authorize(Roles = Roles.Admin)], Roles.ModeratorOrAdmin, IsInRoleAsync(user, Roles.X) — with zero magic role literals in auth decisions, so role vocabulary cannot drift across controllers.

add.header: Layered ban enforcement with session revocation
add.description: Bans are enforced at token mint (login/external), at refresh rotation (TokenService), and at write controllers, and a ban revokes all active refresh tokens (ModerationController.Ban) so a banned session cannot survive on a still-valid token.

add.header: Authorization delegated cleanly into service layer
add.description: SoftDelete computes canModerate from roles in the controller and passes it to MessageService.SoftDeleteAsync, which enforces author-or-moderator and returns an Authorized flag the controller maps to Forbid() — auth logic lives with the data operation rather than scattered conditionals.

add.header: Public reads are an intentional forum boundary
add.description: Read endpoints (room messages, threads, highlights, room list, presence) are deliberately unauthenticated and thread the nullable User.GetUserId() through for per-user enrichment; every mutating endpoint carries [Authorize]. The split is consistent, not accidental.

add.header: Ban-check asymmetry across write siblings
add.description: PostMessage/Upvote/Report guard on user.IsBanned but RemoveUpvote/SoftDelete do not; the omission is defensible but undocumented, forcing per-endpoint reasoning instead of a single trusted rule.

add.header: ChatHub unauthenticated despite JWT-from-query wiring
add.description: The SignalR hub has no [Authorize] yet Program.cs adds access_token-from-query handling for /hub; safe today because the hub only carries presence and content-free change pings, but the wiring and the boundary disagree.

add.header: No old/new API coexistence in frontend or backend
add.description: Single fetch HTTP client, single SignalR realtime transport, all functional + all-TypeScript frontend, no axios/moment dual libs, no [Obsolete] symbols, no sync-over-async (.Result/.Wait) shims, and no V1/V2 parallel paths — a developer is never left guessing which pattern to extend.

add.header: Supabase RLS survives only as test-parity reference
add.description: The prior Supabase RLS authorization design is referenced solely as an integration-test parity target; no RLS/Supabase code remains in production, so the port to C# authorization is complete rather than half-finished.

add.header: RootMessageId==Guid.Empty legacy-row backfill convention
add.description: Pre-ThreadMetadata-migration rows have an empty RootMessageId, so MessageReader/MessageService/HighlightsService compute the effective root on the fly via one consistent convention (documented in ResolveRootAsync); intentional bounded backward-compat, but the logic is repeated across four call sites instead of being centralized.

add.header: Feature-folder layout with namespace-directory parity
add.description: Backend organizes by concern (Auth/, Data/, Domain/, Email/, Features/{Messages,Moderation,Rooms}/, Realtime/, Storage/) with namespaces matching folders one-to-one, so placement is predictable from structure alone.

add.header: Pure dependency-free Domain/ core
add.description: Domain/ has avg_fan_in 21 and avg_fan_out 0 and is imported by every feature directory — a correctly-placed high-fan-in core that anchors the dependency graph rather than a straggler.

add.header: Strictly unidirectional cross-directory coupling
add.description: coupling_matrix shows only downstream→upstream-free, non-reciprocal edges (Features→Domain/Data/Auth/Realtime/Storage), so boundary placement creates no cyclic or backward-dependency hazards.

add.header: Controllers colocated in feature slices
add.description: Controllers live beside their feature service/DTOs (Features/Messages/, Auth/, Realtime/) as a uniform vertical-slice convention rather than a flat MVC Controllers/ folder.

add.header: Empty Controllers/ scaffolding straggler
add.description: server/Sheshi.Api/Controllers/ is an empty leftover from the webapi template whose name contradicts the feature-folder controller convention the codebase actually follows.

add.header: Domain/ mixes entities with static text utilities
add.description: Slug.cs and Text.cs are static normalization helpers sharing the Domain/ folder with EF persistence entities — a minor cohesion blur, mitigated by both being broadly-imported domain-text helpers.

add.header: All wiring via DI container, no import-time side effects
add.description: Program.cs registers every cross-component dependency in the service provider and runs migration/seed after Build() in a created scope, so object creation order is framework-owned rather than import-order dependent. No class-scope DB/file/network calls exist anywhere in the app's boot path.

add.header: Config read at runtime via IOptions/IConfiguration, not at import time
add.description: Services consume JwtOptions/StorageOptions through IOptions<T> and seeding reads SeedAdmin/* via IConfiguration at runtime, keeping startup test-overridable (ApiFactory) and free of fragile module-scope environment reads. The single Environment.GetEnvironmentVariable call is design-time-only EF tooling with a localhost fallback.

add.header: Static state limited to immutable constants and sync primitives
add.description: Every static field is either an immutable constant/lookup table (SeedRooms, Extensions, Interval, CacheTtl) or a synchronization primitive (HighlightsService.SnapshotGate). There is no mutable shared static state whose creation order matters across modules, and the lone singleton PresenceTracker is lock-guarded and self-contained.

add.header: Uniform per-feature controller/service/reader/DTO shape
add.description: Every backend feature (Messages, Rooms, Moderation, Auth) follows the same controller-maps-HTTP, service-runs-commands, reader-runs-queries, DTO-records layout, so learning one feature predicts the rest.

add.header: Consistent Result-record command idiom
add.description: Service write operations return small sealed Result records with named factory methods (Failed/Created/NotFound/Forbidden), giving callers a predictable, self-documenting success/failure contract across the codebase.

add.header: Batch-load-then-GetValueOrDefault enrichment recipe
add.description: DTO projection consistently pre-loads related counts/authors in a few grouped queries and resolves them with GetValueOrDefault, avoiding N+1 and keeping enrichment code structurally identical across MessageEnricher, RoomService, and Moderation analytics.

add.header: Data-driven frontend api and admin UI
add.description: api.ts expresses each endpoint as a one-line typed wrapper over a single dispatch/refresh/error path, and Moderation.tsx renders from arrays (statCards, AdminDataTable rows) with an action-dispatch handler rather than duplicated blocks.

add.header: String-literal error mapping in MessagesController
add.description: CreateMessageAsync branches on magic error strings (ROOM_NOT_FOUND, PARENT_NOT_FOUND, PARENT_ROOM_MISMATCH) instead of the structured boolean result shape its sibling endpoints use, the one spot where the command-result idiom is slightly inconsistent.

add.header: Exact DTO/TS parity under one global JSON policy
add.description: Every REST DTO record maps field-for-field to its frontend/src/types.ts counterpart, and Program.cs applies SnakeCaseLower property/dictionary/enum policies once for the whole MVC pipeline, so consumers can trust a single, uniform wire contract. ModelParityTests and CoreApiTests pin FK/index and JSON-name parity.

add.header: Honest discriminated Result records over null overloading
add.description: Service commands return named Result records (MessageCreateResult, DeleteMessageResult, ReportMessageResult, CreateRoomResult) with explicit Failed/NotFound/Forbidden/Created factories, and controllers translate them to precise HTTP statuses, so every failure mode is part of the stated contract rather than a hidden null.

add.header: SignalR boundary bypasses the global snake_case contract
add.description: AddSignalR() is left unconfigured, so hub events (presence, message_changed) serialize PresenceDto/MessageChangeDto in SignalR's default camelCase while the identical DTOs over REST are snake_case; the frontend masks this by reading both casings, signalling the realtime wire contract is ambiguous and untested.

add.header: Cycle-guarded parent/tree traversals
add.description: ResolveRootAsync, HighlightsService.LoadStatsAsync, and BuildReplyNodes all walk parent chains or trees with explicit `seen` HashSet guards or bounded recursion, so malformed/cyclic data cannot loop forever — the most logic-dense code is also the most carefully bounded.

add.header: Concurrency-safe write paths via row-count guards
add.description: Token rotation (TokenService) and vote insertion (MessageService) use ExecuteUpdate/ON CONFLICT with affected-row checks instead of read-then-write, making concurrent access deterministic without obscuring the happy path.

add.header: TryParseStatus re-derives its return value
add.description: ModerationController.TryParseStatus assigns `parsed` from a switch but computes its boolean return by re-running the same lowercased string-set comparison, duplicating the valid-value list and the ToLowerInvariant() call; cosmetic but the only avoidable redundancy in the dimension.

add.header: Nullable RT and TS strict both enabled and respected
add.description: Backend compiles with <Nullable>enable</Nullable> and the SPA with strict:true; nullability is enforced by the compiler rather than convention, so annotations can be trusted as runtime documentation.

add.header: Nullable return types honestly model not-found paths
add.description: Service/reader methods return T? (AuthResponse?, MessageDto?, ThreadDto?, RoomDto?) on miss and every caller null-checks — return types never lie about the null path.

add.header: Null-suppression operators are guarded or idiomatic
add.description: Every ParentId!.Value is preceded by a `ParentId is not null` filter and EF navigation `= null!` is the framework-standard pattern; the suppressions compensate for lambda flow-narrowing limits, not for unsound assumptions.

add.header: Frontend free of any; casts are idiomatic
add.description: No `any` in the SPA; the only casts are `as const`, `as CSSProperties` for CSS custom properties, typed-fetch `as Promise<T>`, and unavoidable localStorage deserialization (the latter guarded with Array.isArray + type predicate in loadSavedIds).

add.header: Redundant non-null assertions in feeds.tsx
add.description: props.thread! on feeds.tsx:192/209 follows an early-return guard that already narrows the value, and sits beside unguarded accesses — leftover assertions that flow analysis disproves.
